content.problemStatement:
Given a binary tree, print the level order traversal of its nodes. (i.e., from left to right, on each level).

For example, the level order traversal of the following binary tree is: 

1 

2 3 

4 5




Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer NNN — the number of nodes in the binary tree.
Next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contain three space separated characters pi,ci,Rip_i, c_i, R_ipi​,ci​,Ri​, describing the tree with edge informations - pip_ipi​ is an integer denoting the parent node of the iiith edge, cic_ici​ is child node, and the letter RiR_iRi​ denotes whether the child is left child or right child, it's value is `L` if cic_ici​ is left child of pip_ipi​, else `R`.


Output Format
Output on the L separate lines, NNN space separated integers - its nodes in the level order traversal of the given tree, where L is total no. of levels in the given tree.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer N, the number of nodes in the binary tree. Next N-1 lines contain three space-separated values: pi, ci, and Ri, describing the tree with edge information - pi is an integer denoting the parent node of the ith edge, ci is the child node, and the letter Ri denotes whether the child is the left child or the right child, it's value is 'L' if ci is the left child of pi, else 'R'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the level order traversal of the binary tree on separate lines, with nodes on the same depth printed on the same line separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The tree has root 1. Level 0: 1. Level 1: nodes 2 and 3. Level 2: nodes 4 and 5.
Root 1 with left child 2 and right child 3. Levels are printed accordingly.
A single-node tree consists only of the root.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we use a Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm to traverse the binary tree level by level. We start by finding the root of the tree, which is the node that is not a child of any other node. Then, we use a queue to keep track of the nodes at each level. We iterate through the queue, printing the value of each node and adding its children to the queue. This process continues until the queue is empty, at which point we have traversed all levels of the tree.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the number of nodes in the binary tree.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the nodes of the binary tree and the queue used for BFS.
